policy.title: RBI Issues Final Guidelines on Climate Risk Management for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI released final guidelines mandating SCBs to integrate climate risk into ICAAP by April 2027, with mandatory climate stress testing for top-50 banks by exposure. Guidelines specify minimum 30-year projection horizons for physical risk and require board-level climate risk committees.

policy.title: SEBI Expands BRSR Core to Top 500 Listed Companies with Assurance Mandate
policy.description: SEBI extended mandatory BRSR Core reporting with reasonable assurance to top 500 companies by market cap from FY27, up from top 150. Nine KPIs including Scope 1-2 emissions and water intensity now require third-party verification.

policy.title: IRDAI Releases Exposure Draft on Index-Based Insurance Products for Climate Perils
policy.description: IRDAI's exposure draft permits insurers to file parametric products for flood, cyclone, and heat events with pre-approved trigger indices. Draft mandates minimum 0.85 correlation between index and actual losses, with 15-day payout windows.

policy.title: Basel Committee Finalizes Pillar 3 Climate Risk Disclosure Framework
policy.description: BCBS finalized standardized Pillar 3 disclosure templates for climate-related financial risks, effective January 2028. Banks must disclose financed emissions for top sectors and physical risk exposures by geography with granular hazard breakdowns.

policy.title: MCA Announces Phased ISSB-Aligned Indian Sustainability Standards Roadmap
policy.description: Ministry of Corporate Affairs confirmed adoption of ISSB S1/S2-aligned Indian Sustainability Reporting Standards for large unlisted companies from FY28. Standards will require climate scenario analysis consistent with 1.5°C and 3°C pathways.

policy.title: NGFS Releases Phase V Climate Scenarios with Enhanced Physical Risk Granularity
policy.description: NGFS Phase V scenarios introduce sub-national physical risk projections for 47 countries including state-level India data. New acute risk layers cover compound events and include updated damage functions reflecting 2024-25 loss data.

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence Partners with HDFC Bank for Physical Risk Assessment Platform
competitors.description: Jupiter announced a multi-year partnership with HDFC Bank to provide asset-level flood and cyclone risk scoring for the bank's corporate loan book. The deal reportedly covers 50,000+ commercial properties across coastal India.

competitors.title: Sprih Raises $12M Series A to Expand Climate Risk Module for Indian Banks
competitors.description: Bangalore-based Sprih closed $12M Series A led by Sequoia India to build physical risk analytics for NBFC lending portfolios. Company claims 15 NBFC clients and plans to hire 40 climate data scientists.

competitors.title: Moody's Launches India-Specific Physical Risk Scores for Banking Sector
competitors.description: Moody's released localized physical climate risk scores covering 200+ Indian districts with granular flood, drought, and heat stress data. Product integrates with Moody's credit risk models for loan-level PD adjustments.

competitors.title: XDI Expands Gross Asset Value at Risk Database to Cover 5 Million Indian Properties
competitors.description: Australian climate analytics firm XDI expanded its physical risk database to include 5 million Indian commercial and industrial assets. Coverage includes riverine flood, coastal inundation, and extreme heat projections to 2100.

competitors.title: CarbonMint Partners with Axis Bank for Agri-Loan Climate Scoring
competitors.description: CarbonMint signed partnership with Axis Bank to provide satellite-based crop health and climate vulnerability scores for agricultural loan underwriting. Pilot covers 100,000 farmer loans in Maharashtra and Karnataka.

competitors.title: Watershed Raises $200M Series D, Announces Emerging Markets Expansion
competitors.description: Watershed closed $200M Series D at $2.8B valuation, announcing plans to expand carbon accounting platform to India and Southeast Asia by Q4 2026. Company will establish data partnerships with local emission factor providers.

research.title: Physical Climate Risk in Indian Banking: Sectoral Exposure and Credit Implications
research.description: CRISIL analysis finds 18% of Indian bank lending exposed to high physical climate risk sectors, with coastal infrastructure and agriculture portfolios showing potential 15-40 bps PD elevation under RCP 4.5 by 2035. Report recommends granular hazard mapping at PIN code level.

research.title: Scaling Parametric Insurance in Emerging Markets: Lessons from South Asia
research.description: Swiss Re estimates India's parametric insurance protection gap at $8-12B annually for flood and cyclone events. Report highlights basis risk as key adoption barrier and recommends hybrid products combining parametric triggers with traditional coverage.

research.title: ESG Data Gaps in Emerging Markets: Implications for Sustainable Finance Flows
research.description: IFC study documents that only 23% of listed companies in South Asia provide decision-useful climate data versus 67% in developed markets. Report quantifies a $340B annual sustainable investment gap attributable partly to data deficiencies.

research.title: Climate Scenarios for Financial Risk Assessment in Asia-Pacific
research.description: UNEP FI pilot with 12 Asia-Pacific banks tested physical risk stress scenarios, finding median portfolio value-at-risk of 4.2% under hot-house scenario by 2050. Indian participating banks showed highest concentration risk in water-stressed agriculture lending.

research.title: Index-Based Flood Insurance: Trigger Design and Basis Risk Mitigation
research.description: Munich Re technical paper proposes multi-index trigger frameworks combining satellite precipitation, river gauge, and soil moisture data to reduce basis risk below 10% for South Asian flood products. Validates approach against 2024 Kerala and Gujarat flood events.

research.title: Integrating Climate Risk into Credit Risk Models: Methodological Considerations
research.description: BIS working paper reviews approaches for embedding physical and transition risk into PD/LGD models, concluding that sector-geography interaction effects are underweighted in current frameworks. Recommends minimum 10-year forward-looking horizons for meaningful risk differentiation.

ourRead.headline: RBI's final climate guidelines and Jupiter's HDFC deal signal that India's climate risk market is entering execution phase — StepChange must accelerate bank pilots now.
body: This fortnight's signals converge on a singular theme: India's climate risk infrastructure buildout has shifted from consultation to implementation. The regulatory scaffolding is now in place, global players are making concrete market entry moves, and research institutions are producing India-specific methodologies that validate our core technical approach.

The RBI's final climate risk guidelines (policy-1) are the most consequential development for StepChange in 2026. The April 2027 ICAAP integration deadline and mandatory stress testing for top-50 banks creates an 18-month implementation window where Indian banks must procure physical risk analytics. The 30-year projection horizon requirement directly maps to our hazard modelling capabilities. Critically, the guidelines favor granular, asset-level approaches over sectoral proxies — this is our differentiation against simpler ESG data providers.

Jupiter's HDFC partnership (comp-1) and Moody's India-specific product launch (comp-3) confirm that well-resourced global players see the same opportunity we do. However, their approaches carry vulnerabilities: Jupiter's global models require expensive localization, while Moody's district-level granularity may be too coarse for asset-level lending decisions. Sprih's $12M raise (comp-2) is more directly competitive — they're building for NBFCs, which we've deprioritized. Our response should be to lock in pilot agreements with 3-4 PSU banks in Q2 where our PIN code-level hazard data and local model validation provide defensible advantages.

The CRISIL report (research-1) and BIS working paper (research-6) provide immediate ammunition for our sales conversations. CRISIL's 15-40 bps PD elevation finding quantifies the credit risk materiality that bank risk committees need to justify procurement. The BIS recommendation for sector-geography interaction effects validates our approach of combining hazard exposure with borrower industry characteristics. Meanwhile, the Munich Re paper (research-5) on multi-index flood triggers directly informs our parametric product design work with partner insurers.

Watch for: IRDAI's final parametric guidelines expected by May — the 0.85 correlation threshold in the draft is workable but tight. Action item: Schedule briefings with at least two PSU bank CROs this week using the CRISIL PD impact numbers as the hook, before Jupiter's HDFC deal narrative dominates procurement conversations.
